questions.prompt: Which statement about tuning (PID/filter fine-tuning) is most correct?
options: It's a mandatory step that must be done before flying every new drone
Modern default settings are usually good; tuning is for solving a problem or perfecting an already good flight
It's only needed on racing drones and never needed for freestyle
Tuning always slows the drone down
questions.explanation: Stock settings are already good for most drones. Tuning isn't a mandatory step done blindly, but a fine adjustment you turn to when there's a symptom/problem.

questions.prompt: When you see vibration/oscillation on the drone, what should you do before starting to tune?
options: Immediately double the P value
First rule out mechanical issues (bent/cracked prop, loose motor screw, unbalanced part, loose frame)
Change the battery
Turn off all the filters
questions.explanation: Most vibrations that look like a 'PID problem' are actually mechanical. Before trying to fix it in software, make sure the hardware is clean and solid.

questions.prompt: What is the Blackbox log good for in tuning?
options: It records the drone's video more clearly
It extends the battery's life
It logs flight data (gyro, motor output, command); so you can tune by measuring and seeing (e.g. setpoint vs gyro, vibration frequency) instead of 'feeling' it
It reduces the drone's weight
questions.explanation: Blackbox records what happened and when, thousands of times per second. This turns tuning from a guessing game into work done by measuring and observing (with data).

questions.prompt: Which statement is most correct about the trade-off between filtering and latency?
options: The more filters you add, the better the drone flies
Filters have no cost
Every filter cleans up noise but adds latency to the signal; the goal isn't the most filtering, but the least filtering that gets the job done
Filters only affect video quality, not flight
questions.explanation: Filtering cleans up noise, but every filter adds a little latency and can make the drone feel sluggish. The goal is enough cleanup with as little filtering as possible.

questions.prompt: What is the practical approach to follow when tuning?
options: Changing P, I, D, and filters together in big steps at the same time
Changing one variable at a time in small steps and testing; logging the results and keeping a backup to revert to
Cranking all values to maximum without testing anything
Making one change and never touching it again
questions.explanation: Change one thing at a time in small steps; test each change and compare (with Blackbox), and back up a working tune so you can revert if it gets worse.
